selected_skills.trigger_context: Use when a PR changes cache behavior, cached reads, mutation paths, update
selected_skills.trait_instruction: Use when a PR changes cache behavior, cached reads, mutation paths, update

focus_areas: Cache invalidation on user update paths
Stale-read risk between database and Redis cache
Behavioral impact of removing cache eviction in shared user data flows
Callers/routes/services that may read cached user records after mutation
specific_checks: Verify whether `src/cache/userCache.ts:updateUser` is expected to invalidate `user:${userId}` after `db.users.update`.
Trace all read paths for user data to confirm whether `user:${userId}` can remain populated with stale data after an update.
Check whether any compensating mechanism exists (write-through cache update, TTL-only strategy, versioned keys, async refresh) that makes removal of `cache.del` safe.
Review routes and middleware that may depend on fresh user state immediately after update, especially `src/routes/users.ts`, `src/routes/profile.ts`, and `src/middleware/auth.ts`.
Confirm whether tests cover update-then-read behavior for cached users; if not, recommend a regression test for stale reads after `updateUser`.
Assess whether this change can cause authorization/profile/account data inconsistencies if cached user attributes are used after mutation.
risk_profile: Stale cached user records after DB updates
Read-after-write inconsistency across routes and middleware
Hidden correctness regressions if cache is shared across request paths

focus_areas: User-related module interactions
Impact on API correctness for profile/auth/user endpoints
Test coverage gaps around cache-backed user retrieval
specific_checks: Inspect `src/cache/accountCache.ts`, `src/cache/userCache.ts`, and user DB accessors for assumptions about cache eviction on mutation.
Review whether auth/session/profile flows consume cached user objects and could expose outdated roles, status, or profile fields.
Check for missing or outdated tests in `tests/users.test.ts` covering update followed by immediate fetch.
Flag any mismatch between repository conventions and this change, especially if other cache update helpers still invalidate keys after writes.
risk_profile: User-visible stale profile data
Potential stale authorization/account state
Regression risk due to absent integration tests

focus_areas: Cache invalidation on user/account update paths
Stale-read risk between database and Redis-backed user caches
Behavioral impact of removing cache eviction in shared user/profile/auth flows

specific_checks: Verify whether `src/cache/userCache.ts:updateUser` should invalidate or refresh `user:${userId}` after `db.users.update`.
Inspect `src/cache/accountCache.ts`, `src/cache/userCache.ts`, and related user DB accessors for assumptions that mutations evict or rewrite cached entries.

Read-after-write inconsistency across routes, middleware, and auth/profile flows
User-visible stale profile data or stale authorization/account state
Hidden correctness regressions due to missing integration tests or inconsistent cache conventions

hypothetical_bugs: Stale `user:${userId}` entries after `updateUser` now persist in Redis, causing update-then-read flows to return pre-update profile/account fields until TTL expiry or manual eviction.

Immediate read-after-write regressions in shared paths like `routes/users`, `routes/profile`, or `middleware/auth` if they rely on cached user fetches right after mutation; this can surface as stale role/status/auth/account state and incorrect authorization behavior.

Missing regression coverage for cache consistency: tests may still pass if they hit DB directly, leaving untested scenarios where repository conventions expect write invalidation and other cache helpers still evict on mutation, creating inconsistent behavior across user/account flows.

added_reviewers: Security/Concurrency Triage Reviewer for Cache Mutations

focus_areas: Cache key parity between getters and mutators
Read-after-write correctness for user/account cache helpers
Evidence-backed findings limited to changed or directly referenced code
Regression test expectations for stale-cache scenarios
specific_checks: Only report a bug if you can cite the exact getter key format and the exact mutator invalidation/write key format from the diff or directly adjacent referenced code; quote both literal key strings in the finding.

Do not mention routes, middleware, auth flows, or other files unless they are present in the diff or explicitly provided in context. If broader impact is uncertain, state it as a risk hypothesis, not a confirmed bug.

For every cache invalidation finding, verify one of: delete uses the same key as read path, write-through refresh updates the same key, versioned keys make stale entries unreachable, or TTL-only behavior is explicitly intended and documented. If none is evidenced, flag the inconsistency.
Do not invent line numbers or files. Reference only files and line numbers visible in the review context; if exact lines are uncertain, omit the line number.

When suggesting a fix, require that it preserves immediate read-after-write correctness by either deleting the exact read key or overwriting that exact key with the post-update value. Do not present vague 'invalidate cache' guidance without naming the concrete key.
Require at least one test expectation per finding that seeds the exact cache key used by the getter, performs the mutation, and verifies the next getter call returns fresh data.
If the diff is narrow and only supports cache-consistency findings, explicitly avoid speculating about security, concurrency, or performance bugs without concrete evidence in the changed code.
risk_profile: Stale cached user/account records after DB updates
Read-after-write inconsistency caused by mismatched cache keys
Grounding risk from citing non-diff files or unsupported broader flows
Regression risk when tests do not exercise seeded-cache update-then-read behavior

reviewer_architecture_before.persona_name: Security/Concurrency Triage Reviewer for Cache Mutations
focus_areas: Whether the diff contains any actual security-sensitive or concurrency-sensitive behavior
Atomicity and race implications of cache update patterns when evidenced by code
Avoiding rubric-driven hallucinations outside the available patch scope
specific_checks: First determine whether the patch contains authentication, authorization, secret handling, tenant isolation, or externally influenced cache-key construction. If none are present in the diff, explicitly record 'no grounded security finding in scope' rather than forcing a security issue.

Only raise concurrency concerns if the code shows a concrete non-atomic sequence whose correctness depends on ordering, such as read-modify-write, dual writes, or async fire-and-forget behavior. Generic 'race condition' claims are forbidden without code evidence.

If suggesting a stronger fix than simple invalidation (for example write-through refresh), note any evidenced tradeoff such as extra DB read or possible stale overwrite, and tie it to the actual code path.
Do not broaden scope to unrelated modules to satisfy category coverage. The absence of grounded security/performance findings is acceptable and should be stated plainly.
When no additional issue class is supported by the diff, add a concise note that review confidence is limited to cache correctness and key consistency in the touched files.
risk_profile: Hallucinated security or concurrency findings driven by rubric pressure
Overstated performance/concurrency claims without patch evidence
Missed opportunity to explicitly state out-of-scope categories are unsupported by the diff

hypothetical_bugs: Getter/mutator cache-key mismatch causing stale reads: the diff removes `await cache.del(`user:${userId}`)`, so if any directly adjacent getter reads via the literal key `user:${userId}`, updates will no longer invalidate that exact read key and immediate read-after-write correctness will break.

Mutation now has no evidenced cache update semantics at all: after `await db.users.update(userId, data);`, there is neither delete nor write-through refresh for the previously used literal key `user:${userId}`. If tests seed `user:${userId}` before calling `updateUser`, the next getter may return stale cached data until TTL expiry.

Regression risk from stale-cache scenarios no longer being covered: any existing or missing test should explicitly seed the exact getter key `user:${userId}`, perform `updateUser(userId, data)`, then verify the next read does not return the old cached value. Without that, this deletion-removal can silently ship a read-after-write inconsistency bug.
Stale-read/cache-invalidation bug: `updateUser` no longer deletes `user:${userId}` after writing to the DB, so subsequent reads can return outdated user data indefinitely until TTL/eviction.

Cache-key consistency edge case: if reads for this entity rely on the exact `user:${userId}` key, removing invalidation here breaks write/read coherence; reviewer should verify whether any alternate keys (e.g. profile/list/lookup aliases) also depend on this update path and now remain stale too.

No grounded security finding in scope: this patch does not itself show authentication, authorization, secret handling, tenant isolation, or externally influenced cache-key construction. The main supported risk is cache correctness/key consistency in touched files, so avoid inventing unsupported security/concurrency/performance issues.

iterations.final_synthesized_review_summary: 1 comments (High:1). The review found one concrete, high-severity logic regression in `src/cache/userCache.ts`: the user update path no longer invalidates or refreshes the same cache key used by reads. This can expose stale user data immedia
iterations.evaluator_comments: This is a strong, grounded review: it identifies a plausible correctness regression, points to a specific file/line, explains the stale-cache impact clearly, and gives concrete fixes plus solid regression tests. The main limitation is completeness/comprehensiveness: it only covers one correctness issue and does not assess whether there are additional concerns in the change (for example error handling, cache/db consistency edge cases, serialization/TTL behavior, or any security/performance implications). Also, because the underlying diff is not shown here, the confidence in accuracy depends on the code actually using the same `user:${userId}` key on reads and not already handling invalidation elsewhere.
